review_notes: The functions provided are generally well-aligned with the principle of least functionality as described in NIST 800-171 control 3.4.6. They map correctly to Kubernetes security features that enforce minimal functionality and access. The naming conventions are mostly followed, but there are minor improvements that could enhance clarity and consistency.
naming_improvements: Consider renaming 'k8s_admission_controller_pod_security_enabled' to 'k8s_admission_controller_security_policies_enforced' for clarity on the enforcement aspect.
title: 3_4_6: 3.4.6 Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring organizational systems to provide only essential capabilities
step1_reasoning: The control 3.4.6 focuses on employing the principle of least functionality, which aligns with several Kubernetes security practices. In Kubernetes, this can be enforced through RBAC by ensuring roles and permissions are limited to what is necessary (k8s_rbac_least_privilege_enforcement). NetworkPolicies can be used to restrict ingress and egress traffic, ensuring that only essential communication paths are open (k8s_networkpolicy_default_deny_ingress). Pod Security Standards can enforce restrictions on pod capabilities, such as running as non-root, which aligns with limiting unnecessary functionality (k8s_pod_security_standard_restricted). Additionally, admission controllers can enforce security policies at the time of pod creation (k8s_admission_controller_pod_security_enabled).
